## NEURAL TIME-DEPENDENT PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a crucial role in studying a vast number of problems in science and engineering. Numerically solving nonlinear and/or highdimensional PDEs is frequently a challenging task. Inspired by the traditional finite difference and finite elements methods and emerging advancements in machine learning, we propose a sequence-to-sequence learning (Seq2Seq) framework called Neural-PDE, which allows one to automatically learn governing rules of any timedependent PDE system from existing data by using a bidirectional LSTM encoder, and predict the solutions in next _n_ time steps. One critical feature of our proposed framework is that the Neural-PDE is able to simultaneously learn and simulate all variables of interest in a PDE system. We test the Neural-PDE by a range of examples, from one-dimensional PDEs to a multi-dimensional and nonlinear complex fluids model. The results show that the Neural-PDE is capable of learning the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators defining the initial-boundary-value problem of a PDE system without the knowledge of the specific form of the PDE system. In our experiments, the Neural-PDE can efficiently extract the dynamics within 20 epochs training and produce accurate predictions. Furthermore, unlike the traditional machine learning approaches for learning PDEs, such as CNN and MLP, which require great quantity of parameters for model precision, the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The research of time-dependent partial differential equations (PDEs) is regarded as one of the most important disciplines in applied mathematics. PDEs appear ubiquitously in a broad spectrum of fields including physics, biology, chemistry, and finance, to name a few. Despite their fundamental importance, most PDEs can not be solved analytically and have to rely on numerical solving methods. Developing efficient and accurate numerical schemes for solving PDEs, therefore, has been an active research area over the past few decades (Courant et al., 1967; Osher & Sethian, 1988; LeVeque; Cockburn et al., 2012; Thomas, 2013; Johnson, 2012). Still, devising stable and accurate schemes with acceptable computational cost is a difficult task, especially when nonlinear and(or) high-dimensional PDEs are considered. Additionally, PDE models emerged from science and engineering disciplines usually require huge empirical data for model calibration and validation, and determining the multidimensional parameters in such a PDE system poses another challenge (Peng et al., 2020).

Deep learning is considered to be the state-of-the-art tool in classification and prediction of nonlinear inputs, such as image, text, and speech (Litjens et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2018; LeCun et al., 1998; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Hinton et al., 2012). Recently, considerable efforts have been made to employ deep learning tools in designing data-driven methods for solving PDEs (Han et al., 2018; Long et al., 2018; Sirignano & Spiliopoulos, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019). Most of these approaches are based on fully-connected neural networks (FCNNs), convolutional neural networks(CNNs) and multilayer perceptron (MLP). These neural network structures usually require an increment of the layers to improve the predictive accuracy (Raissi et al., 2019), and subsequently lead to a more complicated model due to the additional parameters. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are one type of neural network architectures. RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current

and previous states and share parameters across all inputs. This idea (Sherstinsky, 2020) of using current and previous step states to calculate the state at the next time step is not unique to RNNs. In fact, it is ubiquitously used in numerical PDEs. Almost all time-stepping numerical methods applied to solve time-dependent PDEs, such as Euler’s, Crank-Nicolson, high-order Taylor and its variance Runge-Kutta (Ascher et al., 1997) time-stepping methods, update numerical solution by utilizing solution from previous steps.

This motivates us to think what would happen if we replace the previous step data in the neural network with numerical solution data to PDE supported on grids. It is possible that the neural network behaves like a time-stepping method, for example, forward Euler’s method yields the numerical solution at a new time point as the current state output (Chen et al., 2018). Since the numerical solution on each of the grid point (for finite difference) or grid cell (for finite element) computed at a set of contiguous time points can be treated as neural network input in the form of one time sequence of data, the deep learning framework can be trained to predict any time-dependent PDEs from the time series data supported on some grids if the bidirectional structure is applied (Huang et al., 2015; Schuster & Paliwal, 1997). In other words, the supervised training process can be regarded as a practice of the deep learning framework to learn the numerical solution from the input data, by learning the coefficients on neural network layers.

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) is a neural network built upon RNNs. Unlike vanilla RNNs, which suffer from losing long term information and high probability of gradient vanishing or exploding, LSTM has a specifically designed memory cell with a set of new gates such as input gate and forget gate. Equipped with these new gates which control the time to preserve and pass the information, LSTM is capable of learning long term dependencies without the danger of having gradient vanishing or exploding. In the past two decades, LSTM has been widely used in the field of natural language processing (NLP), such as machine translation, dialogue systems, question answering systems (Lipton et al., 2015).

Inspired by numerical PDE schemes and LSTM neural network, we propose a new deep learning framework, denoted as Neural-PDE. It simulates multi-dimensional governing laws, represented by time-dependent PDEs, from time series data generated on some grids and predicts the next _n_ time steps data. The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs. The detailed structures of the Neural-PDE and data normalization are introduced in Section 3.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 briefly reviews finite difference method for solving PDEs. Section 3 contains detailed description of designing the Neural-PDE. In Section 4 and Appendix A of the paper, we apply the Neural-PDE to solve four different PDEs, including the 1-dimensional(1D) wave equation, the 2-dimensional(2D) heat equation, and two systems of PDEs: the invicid Burgers’ equations and a coupled Navier Stokes-Cahn Hilliard equations, which widely appear in multiscale modeling of complex fluid systems. We demonstrate the robustness of the Neural-PDE, which achieves convergence within 20 epochs with an admissible mean squared error, even when we add Gaussian noise in the input data.

Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).

## EXAMPLE: INVISCID BURGERS’ EQUATION
Inviscid Burgers’ equation is a classical nonlinear PDE in fluid dynamics. In this example, we consider an invicid Burgers’ equation which has the following hyperbolic form:

and with initial and boundary conditions:

The invicid Burgers’ equation is hard to deal with in numerical PDEs due to the discontinuities (shock waves) in the solutions. We use a upwinding finite difference scheme to create the training data and put the velocity _u, v_ in to the input matrix. Let _δx_ = _δy_ = 10 _[−]_[2] _, δt_ = 10 _[−]_[3] , our empirical results (see Figure 4) show that the Neural-PDE is able to learn the shock waves, boundary conditions and the rules of the equation, and predict _u_ and _v_ simultaneously with an overall MSE of 1 _._ 4018 _×_ 10 _[−]_[5] . The complex fluids system has the following features: the micro-structures such as the molecular configurations, the interaction between different scales and the competition between multi-phase fluids (Hyon et al., 2010). Our Neural-PDE prediction shows that the physical features of _p_ and _φ_ have been successfully captured with an overall MSE: 6 _._ 1631 _×_ 10 _[−]_[7] (see Figure 7).

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we proposed a novel sequence recurrent deep learning framework: Neural-PDE, which is capable of intelligently filtering and learning solutions of time-dependent PDEs. One key innovation of our method is that the time marching method from the numerical PDEs is applied in the deep learning framework, and the neural network is trained to explore the accurate numerical solutions for prediction.

The state-of-the-art researches have shown the promising power of deep learning in solving highdimensional nonlinear problems in engineering, biology and finance with efficiency in computation and accuracy in prediction. However, there are still unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs. For instance, the stability and convergence of the numerical algorithms have been rigorously studied by applied mathematicians. Due to the high nonlinearity of the neural network system and the curse of dimensionality (Hutzenthaler et al., 2019), theorems guiding stability and convergence of solutions predicted by the neural network are to be revealed.

Lastly, it would be helpful and interesting if one can theoretically characterize a numerical scheme from the neural network coefficients and learn the forms or mechanics from the scheme and prediction. We leave these questions for further study.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting conceptual bridge between classical numerical time-stepping schemes and Recurrent Neural Networks (specifically LSTMs) to solve time-dependent PDEs. The manuscript is clearly structured, and the motivation—using parameter-sharing in RNNs to mirror the recursive nature of numerical time-marching methods—is compelling and well-articulated. However, the paper’s primary weakness is a lack of technical rigor in its comparison to current state-of-the-art approaches. While the authors claim that traditional deep learning methods like CNNs require "great quantity of parameters," they fail to adequately address why a Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) is superior to more specialized architectures like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) or Neural Operators (e.g., FNOs), which are now industry standards for these tasks. Furthermore, the abstract asserts that the model learns differential operators without knowledge of the system's form, yet the lack of explicit discussion on the inductive bias of an LSTM versus a learnable stencil (as seen in methods like DeepMind’s MeshGraphNets or Finite Difference Neural Networks) leaves a significant gap in explaining *how* the model actually captures spatial dynamics. To reach publication quality, the authors must strengthen their literature review to address recent advancements in operator learning and provide a more critical analysis of the stability and extrapolation capabilities of their approach when moving beyond the training time horizon.

comments.explanation: The paper fails to define the functional forms of the activation functions (e.g., ReLU, tanh) and the specific parameters/range for the sigmoid normalization. Given the sensitivity of PDE-emulating networks to gradient flow and scaling, these omissions hinder reproducibility and verification of the approach.

The explanation is further obscured by an abrupt cut-off in the upwinding example, leaving the theoretical mapping between the PDE stencil and the recurrent architecture unsupported.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript proposes a Seq2Seq framework using bidirectional LSTMs to approximate the solution operator of time-dependent PDEs from data, asserting that the model can autonomously learn governing dynamics, initial/boundary conditions, and differential operators without prior structural knowledge. While the experimental scope is broad, the paper's quality is undermined by substantial overclaiming and ambiguities in its technical description. The primary issue lies in conflating black-box function approximation with symbolic discovery; describing the Neural-PDE as a method that "learns governing rules" and "extracts dynamics" implies interpretability and equation identification, which a Seq2Seq architecture does not inherently provide, necessitating a clearer distinction between learning a predictive surrogate and discovering mathematical laws. Additionally, the use of a bidirectional encoder for time-stepping predictions raises critical concerns regarding causality and robustness during multi-step rollout, as bidirectional contexts rely on future information unavailable during inference, yet the manuscript offers no discussion on error accumulation, stability guarantees, or how the model mitigates these risks compared to standard unidirectional solvers. The novelty claim is also weakened by insufficient positioning relative to established works on RNN-based PDE solvers and neural operators, and assertions that the method "guarantees accuracy" or applies to "any" PDE are mathematically unsound for unconstrained deep learning approaches. Revisions must temper these claims, rigorously address the implications of bidirectional structures for causal prediction, and provide a more nuanced comparison to existing machine learning schemes for PDEs.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of RNN state dependencies and parameter sharing
comments.quote: RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current and previous states and share parameters across all inputs.
comments.explanation: Initially, this sentence raised a red flag regarding the fundamental mechanics of recurrent networks. Standard RNN formulations compute the hidden state at time $t$ as a function of the *previous* hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and the *current* input $x_t$, not 'current and previous states.' Additionally, the phrase 'share parameters across all inputs' is technically imprecise; RNNs share weights across *time steps* (the recurrence dimension), not across different input samples or spatial grid points. The abstract correctly notes sharing across time steps, so this appears to be a terminology slip in the introduction. Given the paper's core premise hinges on mapping time-stepping numerical schemes to neural architectures, precise language about state updates and parameter sharing is crucial. The authors correctly identify that RNNs leverage past information, but the exact formulation as written could mislead readers about how the recurrence actually operates.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding accuracy guarantees in the introduction
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.
comments.explanation: I initially noted the strong assertion that the bidirectional structure 'thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.' While the authors correctly recognize that bidirectional LSTMs improve spatial context aggregation by attending to both upstream and downstream grid points, the word 'guarantees' is scientifically inaccurate for a purely data-driven, black-box approximation framework. Neural networks do not provide mathematical guarantees of accuracy for arbitrary PDEs; their performance depends on network capacity, optimization dynamics, data distribution, stiffness/nonlinearity of the target equation, and potential approximation errors. The authors later validate accuracy empirically in Section 4, which appropriately grounds the claims. However, framing bidirectional attention as a theoretical guarantee overstates what is actually demonstrated and could mislead readers about the method's reliability on out-of-distribution or highly stiff systems. I recommend tempering 'guarantees' to 'significantly enhances the capacity to capture' or 'empirically improves'.

comments.title: Minor inconsistency between abstract and introduction regarding parameter sharing
comments.quote:
Abstract: "the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm."

Introduction: "RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current and previous states and share parameters across all inputs."
comments.explanation: The abstract correctly states that parameter sharing occurs 'among all time steps,' which aligns with the standard definition of recurrent architectures and supports the claim of reduced complexity. However, the introduction later claims the network 'share[s] parameters across all inputs.' This inconsistency in terminology ('time steps' vs. 'inputs') is confusing because 'inputs' could reasonably be interpreted as different spatial grid points, multiple PDE instances, or batch samples. Since the paper's key advantage relies on temporal recurrence efficiency, unifying the terminology to 'time steps' throughout would improve clarity and prevent misinterpretation of the computational savings mechanism.

comments.title: Incorrect point count for 2D finite difference stencil
comments.quote: The scheme (2.2.7) updates _u[n] i,j_[+1] using four points of _u[n]_ values (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: I initially noticed the claim that the update scheme relies on 'four points'. Checking standard finite difference literature for a 2D parabolic PDE, the explicit Euler method combined with a central difference Laplacian uses a five-point stencil: the center grid point `u_{i,j}^n` plus its four immediate neighbors (`u_{i±1,j}^n`, `u_{i,j±1}^n`). Figure 1 itself visually confirms a cross-shaped 5-point dependency. The text's statement omits the center point, which is mathematically necessary for the time-marching step `u^{n+1} = u^n + Δt(...)`. This appears to be a slight misstatement (likely confusing 'neighboring points' with 'total stencil points'), but it creates unnecessary confusion about the exact update rule and contradicts the referenced figure.

comments.title: Overclaimed permutation invariance of BiLSTM architecture
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that bidirectional processing inherently removes the need to regularize or fix the spatial ordering of grid points in the input matrix. While I understand the intent is to highlight flexibility in grid arrangement, this overstates BiLSTM capabilities. Bidirectional RNNs process sequences from both ends to capture long-range dependencies, but they do not intrinsically learn spatial permutation equivariance. Without positional encodings, graph-based message passing, or explicit training on randomized spatial permutations, a sequence model will still treat different spatial orderings as distinct inputs and may fail to learn locality-invariant features. The claim could mislead readers regarding how spatial information is actually preserved in the latent representation.

comments.explanation: I initially noticed that the time index is placed as a subscript here, which differs from the notation used consistently throughout Section 2 and 3.1 (e.g., $\hat{u}^n_i$). comments.title: Underspecified Data Flow and Ambiguous Spatial Processing Claim
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly recognize that sequence models can capture long-range dependencies, which is a strong point for processing PDE data. However, their claim that the bidirectional structure inherently handles spatial grid dependencies without order regularization puzzled me. I checked how a BiLSTM operates: it processes a fixed sequence dimension step-by-step. If the sequence dimension is time, the network learns temporal dynamics, not spatial stencils. If the sequence dimension is spatial points, the bidirectional structure merely captures correlations along an arbitrary grid ordering, not physical locality. The passage does not specify how the $K \times N$ matrix is actually unrolled or fed into the network to capture neighbor interactions. This ambiguity makes the claim of 'simultaneous spatial processing' misleading and leaves the core methodology underspecified.

comments.title: Questionable Analogy to Numerical Schemes
comments.quote: which indicates the neural network could be seen as a forward Euler method for equation 3.1.7 (Lu et al., 2018). Function $f_2 \equiv \hat{u}_i^n - \nu \frac{\delta x}{\delta t} (\hat{u}_i^n - \hat{u}_{i-1}^n)$ : $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ and the function $f_\theta$ represents the dynamics of the hidden layers in decoder with parameters $\theta$ , and $f_\eta$ specifies the dynamics of the LSTM layer (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Graves & Schmidhuber, 2005) in encoder withe parameters $\eta$ . The function $f_{\theta,\eta}$ simulates the dynamics of the Neural-PDE with paramaters $\theta$ and $\eta$ .
comments.explanation: I appreciate the authors' attempt to ground the deep learning framework in numerical analysis, which is a novel and valuable perspective. However, equating the learned neural update to a forward Euler method is conceptually stretched. Forward Euler requires a fixed $\delta t$ and an explicit differential operator $g(\mathbf{u})$, whereas $f_2$ here is an internal function with free parameters optimized empirically. The network learns an approximate data-driven mapping rather than enforcing the physics of a time-stepping integrator. This overstatement may mislead readers into believing the method inherits the stability or convergence guarantees of traditional numerical schemes, which is not supported by the text.

comments.title: Missing Derivation for Core Theoretical Motivation
comments.quote: Combining equations (3.1.1) and (3.1.6) one may notice that the residual networks, recurrent neural network and also LSTM networks can be regarded as a numerical scheme for solving time-dependent differential equations if more layers are added and smaller time steps are taken. (Chen et al., 2018)
comments.explanation: The authors correctly reference Chen et al. (2018) to motivate using RNNs for differential equations, establishing a useful conceptual bridge. However, the actual combined equation or the explicit mapping between network layers/weights and finite difference coefficients is omitted here. I checked the preceding paragraphs, but the derivation is skipped. While this might be a summary of cited work, the current passage lacks the mathematical steps needed to show *how* adding layers and reducing $\delta t$ transforms the network into a numerical scheme. This insufficient justification weakens the theoretical motivation for the proposed architecture.

comments.title: Misaligned citation regarding the curse of dimensionality and stability theorems
comments.quote: Due to the high nonlinearity of the neural network system and the curse of dimensionality (Hutzenthaler et al., 2019), theorems guiding stability and convergence of solutions predicted by the neural network are to be revealed.
comments.explanation: I initially paused at the causal link drawn between the 'curse of dimensionality' and the need for new stability/convergence theorems. I checked the cited Hutzenthaler et al. (2019) reference and noted that the paper actually demonstrates how deep networks *overcome* the curse of dimensionality for certain PDEs. I acknowledge that the authors correctly identify a valid gap: classical numerical analysis has rigorously established stability and convergence, whereas learning-based PDE solvers currently lack formal guarantees. However, using a citation that highlights overcoming the curse of dimensionality to support the claim that the curse itself is a barrier to stability theorems creates a logical disconnect. The lack of theorems here is primarily due to the complex, highly nonlinear mapping of deep architectures, not the dimensionality curse. I recommend rephrasing to separate the high-dimensional success of NNs from the separate theoretical challenge of proving convergence for learned discretizations.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in proposed future work
comments.quote: Lastly, it would be helpful and interesting if one can theoretically characterize a numerical scheme from the neural network coefficients and learn the forms or mechanics from the scheme and prediction.
comments.explanation: I initially found the phrase 'learn the forms or mechanics from the scheme and prediction' somewhat circular. I checked the surrounding context to understand the authors' intent and realized they are proposing to reverse-engineer the discrete numerical scheme from the trained network weights, which aligns with their earlier claims that RNN structures mimic finite difference stencils. I acknowledge that this is a sensible and valuable future direction for interpretable scientific machine learning. However, the phrasing currently suggests deriving mechanics *from* a scheme that one is supposedly characterizing *from* the coefficients. In standard numerical practice, characterizing a scheme involves analyzing effective local truncation errors or mapping weight matrices to specific stencil coefficients. Clarifying that the goal is to 'extract the discrete scheme's stencil or effective parameters from the learned coefficients' would remove the ambiguity and align better with numerical analysis conventions.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript proposes a Seq2Seq framework using bidirectional LSTMs to approximate the solution operator of time-dependent PDEs from data, asserting that the model can autonomously learn governing dynamics, initial/boundary conditions, and differential operators without prior structural knowledge. While the experimental scope is broad, the paper's quality is undermined by substantial overclaiming and ambiguities in its technical description. The primary issue lies in conflating black-box function approximation with symbolic discovery; describing the Neural-PDE as a method that "learns governing rules" and "extracts dynamics" implies interpretability and equation identification, which a Seq2Seq architecture does not inherently provide, necessitating a clearer distinction between learning a predictive surrogate and discovering mathematical laws. Additionally, the use of a bidirectional encoder for time-stepping predictions raises critical concerns regarding causality and robustness during multi-step rollout, as bidirectional contexts rely on future information unavailable during inference, yet the manuscript offers no discussion on error accumulation, stability guarantees, or how the model mitigates these risks compared to standard unidirectional solvers. The novelty claim is also weakened by insufficient positioning relative to established works on RNN-based PDE solvers and neural operators, and assertions that the method "guarantees accuracy" or applies to "any" PDE are mathematically unsound for unconstrained deep learning approaches. Revisions must temper these claims, rigorously address the implications of bidirectional structures for causal prediction, and provide a more nuanced comparison to existing machine learning schemes for PDEs.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of RNN state dependencies and parameter sharing
comments.quote: RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current and previous states and share parameters across all inputs.
comments.explanation: Initially, this sentence raised a red flag regarding the fundamental mechanics of recurrent networks. Standard RNN formulations compute the hidden state at time $t$ as a function of the *previous* hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and the *current* input $x_t$, not 'current and previous states.' Additionally, the phrase 'share parameters across all inputs' is technically imprecise; RNNs share weights across *time steps* (the recurrence dimension), not across different input samples or spatial grid points. The abstract correctly notes sharing across time steps, so this appears to be a terminology slip in the introduction. Given the paper's core premise hinges on mapping time-stepping numerical schemes to neural architectures, precise language about state updates and parameter sharing is crucial. The authors correctly identify that RNNs leverage past information, but the exact formulation as written could mislead readers about how the recurrence actually operates.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding accuracy guarantees in the introduction
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.
comments.explanation: I initially noted the strong assertion that the bidirectional structure 'thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.' While the authors correctly recognize that bidirectional LSTMs improve spatial context aggregation by attending to both upstream and downstream grid points, the word 'guarantees' is scientifically inaccurate for a purely data-driven, black-box approximation framework. Neural networks do not provide mathematical guarantees of accuracy for arbitrary PDEs; their performance depends on network capacity, optimization dynamics, data distribution, stiffness/nonlinearity of the target equation, and potential approximation errors. The authors later validate accuracy empirically in Section 4, which appropriately grounds the claims. However, framing bidirectional attention as a theoretical guarantee overstates what is actually demonstrated and could mislead readers about the method's reliability on out-of-distribution or highly stiff systems. I recommend tempering 'guarantees' to 'significantly enhances the capacity to capture' or 'empirically improves'.

comments.title: Minor inconsistency between abstract and introduction regarding parameter sharing
comments.quote:
Abstract: "the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm."

Introduction: "RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current and previous states and share parameters across all inputs."
comments.explanation: The abstract correctly states that parameter sharing occurs 'among all time steps,' which aligns with the standard definition of recurrent architectures and supports the claim of reduced complexity. However, the introduction later claims the network 'share[s] parameters across all inputs.' This inconsistency in terminology ('time steps' vs. 'inputs') is confusing because 'inputs' could reasonably be interpreted as different spatial grid points, multiple PDE instances, or batch samples. Since the paper's key advantage relies on temporal recurrence efficiency, unifying the terminology to 'time steps' throughout would improve clarity and prevent misinterpretation of the computational savings mechanism.

comments.title: Incorrect point count for 2D finite difference stencil
comments.quote: The scheme (2.2.7) updates _u[n] i,j_[+1] using four points of _u[n]_ values (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: I initially noticed the claim that the update scheme relies on 'four points'. Checking standard finite difference literature for a 2D parabolic PDE, the explicit Euler method combined with a central difference Laplacian uses a five-point stencil: the center grid point `u_{i,j}^n` plus its four immediate neighbors (`u_{i±1,j}^n`, `u_{i,j±1}^n`). Figure 1 itself visually confirms a cross-shaped 5-point dependency. The text's statement omits the center point, which is mathematically necessary for the time-marching step `u^{n+1} = u^n + Δt(...)`. This appears to be a slight misstatement (likely confusing 'neighboring points' with 'total stencil points'), but it creates unnecessary confusion about the exact update rule and contradicts the referenced figure.

comments.title: Overclaimed permutation invariance of BiLSTM architecture
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that bidirectional processing inherently removes the need to regularize or fix the spatial ordering of grid points in the input matrix. While I understand the intent is to highlight flexibility in grid arrangement, this overstates BiLSTM capabilities. Bidirectional RNNs process sequences from both ends to capture long-range dependencies, but they do not intrinsically learn spatial permutation equivariance. Without positional encodings, graph-based message passing, or explicit training on randomized spatial permutations, a sequence model will still treat different spatial orderings as distinct inputs and may fail to learn locality-invariant features. The claim could mislead readers regarding how spatial information is actually preserved in the latent representation.

comments.title: Underspecified encoder-decoder mapping equations
comments.quote:
The Neural-PDE is an encoder-decoder style sequence model that first maps the input data to a low dimensional latent space that

where ⊕ denotes concatenation and **h** i is the latent embedding of point **a** i under the environment.

    One then decoder, another bi-lstm with a dense layer:

where **W** is the learnable weight matrix in the dense layer.
comments.explanation: I traced the description of the Neural-PDE architecture and found that the core equations for the encoder's projection to a latent space and the decoder's reconstruction step are completely missing from this passage. The prose jumps from 'that [newline] where ⊕ denotes...' and 'One then decoder... [newline] where W is...' without providing the corresponding mathematical mappings. While some extraction loss is expected from PDF OCR, the absence of these formulations leaves critical choices implicit: latent dimension size, activation functions, how batch/sequence dimensions are handled, and the exact decoder topology. This gap makes the method underspecified for reproduction.

comments.title: Typographical error: 'spacial' instead of 'spatial'
comments.quote: We replaceˆ the temporal previous state _**h**[t][−]_[1] with spacial grid value _**h** i−_ 1
comments.explanation: During the upwinding motivation derivation, the text uses 'spacial grid value' instead of the standard mathematical term 'spatial'. This is a minor typographical error that does not affect the mathematical logic or notation flow, but it should be corrected to meet formal publication standards.

comments.title: Notation Inconsistency: Temporal vs Spatial Indexing
comments.quote: $\mathbf{a}_i^N = [\hat{u}_{0i}, \hat{u}_{1i}, \cdots, \hat{u}_{Ni}]$
comments.explanation: I initially noticed that the time index is placed as a subscript here, which differs from the notation used consistently throughout Section 2 and 3.1 (e.g., $\hat{u}^n_i$). I checked the surrounding context to see if the authors intentionally swapped conventions for the latent space representation, but they do not explain or justify the change. While the authors correctly identify that temporal sequences should be fed into the model, flipping from superscript-to-time to subscript-to-time without comment creates a notational conflict that could confuse readers trying to track sequence dimensions against the spatial index $i$.

comments.title: Underspecified Data Flow and Ambiguous Spatial Processing Claim
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly recognize that sequence models can capture long-range dependencies, which is a strong point for processing PDE data. However, their claim that the bidirectional structure inherently handles spatial grid dependencies without order regularization puzzled me. I checked how a BiLSTM operates: it processes a fixed sequence dimension step-by-step. If the sequence dimension is time, the network learns temporal dynamics, not spatial stencils. If the sequence dimension is spatial points, the bidirectional structure merely captures correlations along an arbitrary grid ordering, not physical locality. The passage does not specify how the $K \times N$ matrix is actually unrolled or fed into the network to capture neighbor interactions. This ambiguity makes the claim of 'simultaneous spatial processing' misleading and leaves the core methodology underspecified.

comments.title: Questionable Analogy to Numerical Schemes
comments.quote: which indicates the neural network could be seen as a forward Euler method for equation 3.1.7 (Lu et al., 2018). Function $f_2 \equiv \hat{u}_i^n - \nu \frac{\delta x}{\delta t} (\hat{u}_i^n - \hat{u}_{i-1}^n)$ : $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ and the function $f_\theta$ represents the dynamics of the hidden layers in decoder with parameters $\theta$ , and $f_\eta$ specifies the dynamics of the LSTM layer (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Graves & Schmidhuber, 2005) in encoder withe parameters $\eta$ . The network learns an approximate data-driven mapping rather than enforcing the physics of a time-stepping integrator. This overstatement may mislead readers into believing the method inherits the stability or convergence guarantees of traditional numerical schemes, which is not supported by the text.

comments.title: Contradictory description of variable magnitude
comments.quote: variables such as $\phi \in [0, 1]$ with large magnitude value
comments.explanation: The text states that $\phi$ has a 'large magnitude value'. However, the given definition is $\phi \in [0, 1]$, which is a bounded range with a maximum magnitude of 1. 'Large magnitude' is typically reserved for absolute values significantly greater than 1 (e.g., 10, 100). This creates a logical contradiction in the text. It is possible the author meant 'large spatial variation' or 'significant dynamic range' given the context of normalization, but the phrasing is misleading as written.

comments.title: Scaling argument for normalization seems inconsistent
comments.quote: variable of very small values such as $p \sim 10^{-5}$ ... we normalized the $p$ data with a sigmoid function.
comments.explanation: The text suggests normalization is necessary because $\phi$ (in $[0,1]$) and $p$ ($\sim 10^{-5}$) are on different scales. However, if $p$ is truly $10^{-5}$, applying a sigmoid normalization function ($1 / (1 + e^{-x})$) results in a value of approximately $1.000000000005$, effectively collapsing it to 1.0. This hardly demonstrates a beneficial normalization strategy for scale differences compared to a variable already in $[0,1]$. If the intent was to show the network handling variables with different magnitudes, the specific magnitude cited for $p$ ($10^{-5}$) makes the 'scale discrepancy' argument weak or the normalization choice numerically trivial.

comments.title: Missing hyphen
comments.quote: solving highdimensional nonlinear problems
comments.explanation: The author incorrectly concatenates the adjectives 'high' and 'dimensional' into 'highdimensional'. In academic writing, compound adjectives should be hyphenated to clearly indicate they modify 'nonlinear problems'.

comments.title: Non-standard use of plural noun
comments.quote: The state-of-the-art researches have shown the promising power...
comments.explanation: The word 'research' is typically treated as an uncountable mass noun in this context (e.g., 'state-of-the-art research has shown'). The plural 'researches' is archaic or a typo; it is awkward to say that specific pieces of research are state-of-the-art rather than the collective body of work itself. While not a logic error, it is a grammatical usage error.

comments.title: Repetitive prepositions
comments.quote: unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs
comments.explanation: The preposition 'in' is used twice in close succession. While colloquially acceptable, it reduces stylistic quality. It would be better phrased as 'unresolved issues in the application of deep learning to PDEs' or 'applying deep learning to PDEs'.

comments.title: Ambiguous framing of contributions
comments.quote: However, there are still unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs.
comments.explanation: This sentence appears in the Conclusion, right after stating that state-of-the-art research shows deep learning can solve problems 'with efficiency in computation and accuracy in prediction.' It then asserts there are 'unresolved issues' in applying it to PDEs. Given the paper's title and claims (learning governing rules, accurate predictions, learning differential operators), this broad statement contradicts the implication of the paper's success and creates a subtle cognitive dissonance. It frames the authors' work as solving a specific instance of a field-wide problem, rather than a solution to PDEs themselves.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting concept—employing an LSTM-based sequence-to-sequence framework for learning and predicting time-dependent PDE solutions—and appears to be competently written in terms of structure. However, several critical issues undermine its clarity and contribution. The claims in the abstract are overly broad and imprecise: stating that the model can “automatically learn governing rules of any time-dependent PDE system” and “learn the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators” without knowing the PDE form is a strong assertion that is not justified by the beginning of the text. The introduction does not clearly differentiate Neural-PDE from existing data-driven PDE solvers (e.g., PINNs, neural operators); for example, the observation that RNNs share parameters across time steps is a standard property, not a novel advantage. The description of how the bidirectional LSTM handles spatial correlations in multiple dimensions is vague, and the claim that the method avoids the curse of dimensionality is unsupported. Moreover, the experimental validation is limited to relatively low-dimensional PDEs, and the practical feasibility for truly high-dimensional systems remains unaddressed. The writing, while generally clear, contains some redundant background on RNNs/LSTMs that does not serve to foreground the paper’s specific contribution. To strengthen the paper, the authors must rigorously define what “learning” the PDE entails, provide a detailed comparison with relevant baselines, and temper the claims regarding generality and efficiency.

comments.title: Grid spacing definition sign error
comments.quote: δx =  rac{[x_a] N[-] x[x_b]}{Nx}
comments.explanation: The passage defines δx as (x_a - x_b)/N_x, which is negative if x_a < x_b. The intended spacing for a domain [x_a, x_b] should be (x_b - x_a)/N_x. This sign error would propagate into finite difference formulas, flipping the sign of spatial derivatives. While this may be an OCR artifact (the original likely had (x_b - x_a)/N_x), the text as given contains a mathematical inconsistency. A reader relying on this definition would obtain a negative grid spacing, affecting the numerical scheme.

comments.title: Time step definition garbled
comments.quote: δt = N^{T}
comments.explanation: The expression 'δt = N^{T}' is not mathematically meaningful. The context clearly intends δt = T/N (total time divided by number of time steps). This appears to be an OCR misreading of a fraction. While the correct form can be inferred, the garbled notation could confuse a reader trying to reproduce the discretization.

comments.title: Incomplete upwinding sketch and missing equations in Section 3.1
comments.quote: A simple sketch of the upwinding method for a 1d example of u(x,t): will be:
comments.explanation: The sentence trails off without providing the actual upwinding scheme or the referred equation 3.1.7. This leaves the motivation for replacing temporal hidden states with spatial information incomplete. A reader cannot follow the authors' reasoning for how the neural network mimics the finite difference update without these equations.

comments.title: Notation conflict: h used for both hidden state and spatial neighbor
comments.quote: replace the temporal previous state h[t-1] with spacial grid value h_{i-1}
comments.explanation: Earlier in the section, h[·] denotes the hidden state of the RNN. Here, the authors use h_{i-1} to refer to the numerical solution at the previous spatial grid point, but this symbol was never defined for that purpose. Using the same letter h for two different quantities (hidden state vs. grid value) without clarification creates ambiguity. The intended meaning is likely u_{i-1}^n, not a hidden state.

comments.title: Underspecified Neural-PDE architecture in Section 3.2
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is an encoder-decoder style sequence model that first maps the input data to a low dimensional latent space that ... One then decoder, another bi-lstm with a dense layer:
comments.explanation: The description of the encoder-decoder architecture is incomplete: the sentence 'maps the input data to a low dimensional latent space that' is cut off, and the decoder description lacks concrete equations or dimensions. The phrase 'another bi-lstm with a dense layer' is too vague to specify how the decoder transforms the latent embedding into predictions. This omission prevents a reader from reproducing the model without guessing key design choices.

comments.title: Missing equations for example PDEs
comments.quote: In this example, we consider an invicid Burgers’ equation which has the following hyperbolic form:
comments.explanation: The passage claims to present a specific PDE form but no equation follows. Similarly, the Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes example is introduced with “let’s consider the following 2d Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes system” without showing the equations. Because these examples are central to demonstrating the method, the absence of the governing PDEs and initial/boundary conditions makes it impossible for the reader to reproduce or verify the experiments. Even if the equations appear in figures or elsewhere in the full paper, the passage as provided is critically underspecified.

comments.title: Questionable claim about input order invariance from bidirectional LSTM
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that arbitrary ordering of grid points in the input matrix is permissible due to the bidirectional LSTM. However, the LSTM still processes the sequence in a fixed order; bidirectionality processes both a forward and backward pass but does not inherently make the representation invariant to permutation of the entire list. The spatial relationships between neighboring grid points are lost when the order is scrambled, and it is unclear how the network would learn spatial derivatives without a consistent adjacency structure. Standard practice in similar works (e.g., PDE-Net, PINNs) either preserves spatial ordering or embeds coordinate information. This claim is not justified by the provided context and could mislead readers about the framework's flexibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent data split description
comments.quote: the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing. We will predict the next tp ∈ [31 × δt, 40 × δt] PDE solution by using its previous ttr ∈ [0, 30 × δt]
comments.explanation: The text first mentions a random 80/20 split of the whole dataset, but then describes a fixed prediction scenario using the first 30 time steps for training and the next 10 for testing. These two descriptions are contradictory: a random split across all time series would not guarantee that the test set consists exclusively of time steps 31–40, nor that training uses only steps 0–30. The ambiguity could affect the interpretation of the experimental results, as the reader cannot determine whether the test MSEs reflect interpolation or extrapolation.

comments.title: Underspecified sigmoid normalization for pressure data
comments.quote: we normalized the p data with a sigmoid function.
comments.explanation: The sigmoid function maps values to (0,1), but the pressure data p has very small magnitude (∼10⁻⁵). Simply applying a sigmoid would squash all values to around 0.5, losing fine-scale differences. The authors do not specify whether the normalization is applied before feeding into the network, whether a scaling is used first, or how the predictions are transformed back to physical units. Without this detail, the data preprocessing step is not reproducible.

comments.title: Notation error: N = δt[T] should likely be N = T/δt
comments.quote: Here N = δt[T]
comments.explanation: The expression is nonsensical as written. Given the earlier definition in the paper (N = T/δt), the intended symbol is almost certainly N = T/δt. This is a clear OCR misread, but it would confuse a reader if taken literally. Since it is a real issue in the presented text, it should be corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing about stability and convergence as an example of unresolved issues
comments.quote: However, there are still unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs. For instance, the stability and convergence of the numerical algorithms have been rigorously studied by applied mathematicians.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure initially suggests that the speaker is giving an example of an unresolved issue, but the example itself states that stability and convergence have been rigorously studied. A careful reader will infer from the following sentence that the intended contrast is between traditional numerical algorithms (well-studied) and neural-network-based methods (not yet studied). However, the lack of an explicit contrastive marker (e.g., 'while' or 'whereas') could temporarily confuse a reader into thinking the example contradicts the claim of unresolved issues. The paragraph eventually resolves this, but the phrasing is ambiguous on first reading.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting concept—employing an LSTM-based sequence-to-sequence framework for learning and predicting time-dependent PDE solutions—and appears to be competently written in terms of structure. However, several critical issues undermine its clarity and contribution. The claims in the abstract are overly broad and imprecise: stating that the model can “automatically learn governing rules of any time-dependent PDE system” and “learn the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators” without knowing the PDE form is a strong assertion that is not justified by the beginning of the text. The introduction does not clearly differentiate Neural-PDE from existing data-driven PDE solvers (e.g., PINNs, neural operators); for example, the observation that RNNs share parameters across time steps is a standard property, not a novel advantage. The description of how the bidirectional LSTM handles spatial correlations in multiple dimensions is vague, and the claim that the method avoids the curse of dimensionality is unsupported. Moreover, the experimental validation is limited to relatively low-dimensional PDEs, and the practical feasibility for truly high-dimensional systems remains unaddressed. The writing, while generally clear, contains some redundant background on RNNs/LSTMs that does not serve to foreground the paper’s specific contribution. To strengthen the paper, the authors must rigorously define what “learning” the PDE entails, provide a detailed comparison with relevant baselines, and temper the claims regarding generality and efficiency.

comments.title: Grid spacing definition sign error
comments.quote: δx =  rac{[x_a] N[-] x[x_b]}{Nx}
comments.explanation: The passage defines δx as (x_a - x_b)/N_x, which is negative if x_a < x_b. The intended spacing for a domain [x_a, x_b] should be (x_b - x_a)/N_x. This sign error would propagate into finite difference formulas, flipping the sign of spatial derivatives. While this may be an OCR artifact (the original likely had (x_b - x_a)/N_x), the text as given contains a mathematical inconsistency. A reader relying on this definition would obtain a negative grid spacing, affecting the numerical scheme.

comments.title: Time step definition garbled
comments.quote: δt = N^{T}
comments.explanation: The expression 'δt = N^{T}' is not mathematically meaningful. The context clearly intends δt = T/N (total time divided by number of time steps). This appears to be an OCR misreading of a fraction. While the correct form can be inferred, the garbled notation could confuse a reader trying to reproduce the discretization.

comments.title: Incomplete upwinding sketch and missing equations in Section 3.1
comments.quote: A simple sketch of the upwinding method for a 1d example of u(x,t): will be:
comments.explanation: The sentence trails off without providing the actual upwinding scheme or the referred equation 3.1.7. This leaves the motivation for replacing temporal hidden states with spatial information incomplete. A reader cannot follow the authors' reasoning for how the neural network mimics the finite difference update without these equations.

comments.title: Notation conflict: h used for both hidden state and spatial neighbor
comments.quote: replace the temporal previous state h[t-1] with spacial grid value h_{i-1}
comments.explanation: Earlier in the section, h[·] denotes the hidden state of the RNN. Here, the authors use h_{i-1} to refer to the numerical solution at the previous spatial grid point, but this symbol was never defined for that purpose. Using the same letter h for two different quantities (hidden state vs. grid value) without clarification creates ambiguity. The intended meaning is likely u_{i-1}^n, not a hidden state.

comments.title: Underspecified Neural-PDE architecture in Section 3.2
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is an encoder-decoder style sequence model that first maps the input data to a low dimensional latent space that ... One then decoder, another bi-lstm with a dense layer:
comments.explanation: The description of the encoder-decoder architecture is incomplete: the sentence 'maps the input data to a low dimensional latent space that' is cut off, and the decoder description lacks concrete equations or dimensions. The phrase 'another bi-lstm with a dense layer' is too vague to specify how the decoder transforms the latent embedding into predictions. This omission prevents a reader from reproducing the model without guessing key design choices.

comments.title: Missing equations for example PDEs
comments.quote: In this example, we consider an invicid Burgers’ equation which has the following hyperbolic form:
comments.explanation: The passage claims to present a specific PDE form but no equation follows. Similarly, the Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes example is introduced with “let’s consider the following 2d Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes system” without showing the equations. Because these examples are central to demonstrating the method, the absence of the governing PDEs and initial/boundary conditions makes it impossible for the reader to reproduce or verify the experiments. Even if the equations appear in figures or elsewhere in the full paper, the passage as provided is critically underspecified.

comments.title: Questionable claim about input order invariance from bidirectional LSTM
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that arbitrary ordering of grid points in the input matrix is permissible due to the bidirectional LSTM. However, the LSTM still processes the sequence in a fixed order; bidirectionality processes both a forward and backward pass but does not inherently make the representation invariant to permutation of the entire list. The spatial relationships between neighboring grid points are lost when the order is scrambled, and it is unclear how the network would learn spatial derivatives without a consistent adjacency structure. Standard practice in similar works (e.g., PDE-Net, PINNs) either preserves spatial ordering or embeds coordinate information. This claim is not justified by the provided context and could mislead readers about the framework's flexibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent data split description
comments.quote: the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing. We will predict the next tp ∈ [31 × δt, 40 × δt] PDE solution by using its previous ttr ∈ [0, 30 × δt]
comments.explanation: The text first mentions a random 80/20 split of the whole dataset, but then describes a fixed prediction scenario using the first 30 time steps for training and the next 10 for testing. These two descriptions are contradictory: a random split across all time series would not guarantee that the test set consists exclusively of time steps 31–40, nor that training uses only steps 0–30. The ambiguity could affect the interpretation of the experimental results, as the reader cannot determine whether the test MSEs reflect interpolation or extrapolation.

comments.title: Underspecified sigmoid normalization for pressure data
comments.quote: we normalized the p data with a sigmoid function.
comments.explanation: The sigmoid function maps values to (0,1), but the pressure data p has very small magnitude (∼10⁻⁵). Simply applying a sigmoid would squash all values to around 0.5, losing fine-scale differences. The authors do not specify whether the normalization is applied before feeding into the network, whether a scaling is used first, or how the predictions are transformed back to physical units. Without this detail, the data preprocessing step is not reproducible.

comments.title: Incomplete description of decoder step
comments.quote: One then decoder, another bi-lstm with a dense layer:
comments.explanation: This appears to be a garbled sentence left over from an OCR error or incomplete editing. It is unclear whether an equation or further explanation was intended. The preceding paragraph describes an encoder step (Eq. 3.2.1) and then jumps to “One then decoder…” without providing the decoder update rule. The methodological description of the Neural-PDE architecture is therefore incomplete.

comments.title: Notation error: N = δt[T] should likely be N = T/δt
comments.quote: Here N = δt[T]
comments.explanation: The expression is nonsensical as written. Given the earlier definition in the paper (N = T/δt), the intended symbol is almost certainly N = T/δt. This is a clear OCR misread, but it would confuse a reader if taken literally. Since it is a real issue in the presented text, it should be corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing about stability and convergence as an example of unresolved issues
comments.quote: However, there are still unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs. For instance, the stability and convergence of the numerical algorithms have been rigorously studied by applied mathematicians.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure initially suggests that the speaker is giving an example of an unresolved issue, but the example itself states that stability and convergence have been rigorously studied. A careful reader will infer from the following sentence that the intended contrast is between traditional numerical algorithms (well-studied) and neural-network-based methods (not yet studied). However, the lack of an explicit contrastive marker (e.g., 'while' or 'whereas') could temporarily confuse a reader into thinking the example contradicts the claim of unresolved issues. The paragraph eventually resolves this, but the phrasing is ambiguous on first reading.
